sectionHeader.title: ADHD Coaching
sectionHeader.subtitle: A thousand colorful bricks. One puzzling question.

subsections.title: Prologue: A View from the Rug

defaultContent.text: You’re sitting on the floor, surrounded by a thousand colorful bricks. You know exactly what you want to build — a 1,254-piece masterpiece. (Well, 1,251. You lost three pieces somewhere.)

You've wanted to build for weeks. You opened the box three times before. Today is the day! But you don't start. Not because you don't know how. Not because you don’t want to. Duh!
Yet you just sit there. Completely aware of everything you need to do, and yet completely unable to make yourself do it.

That gap, between knowing, wanting, and doing, is one of the most disorienting parts of ADHD.
And it's exactly where coaching lives.

adhdContent.text: ⚡In an ADHD brain, the wiring between thinking and doing is frayed. Sometimes the spark carries, sometimes not, and rarely is the connection consistent.
⚡It's not a lack of willpower or intelligence. Most certainly not laziness. It’s a bio-mechanical failure in the delivery system of your own intention.
⚡It’s frustrating. It's baffling. It’s invisible.

subsections.note: May wish to add a tooltip for bespoke
subsections.title: What Is ADHD Coaching?

defaultContent.text:
ADHD Coaching is a bespoke partnership.
Bespoke, because each person with ADHD actually experiences different challenges, and to different extents. It focuses on helping you understand better how your brain actually works. And build strategies that work in accordance with it, not despite it.
A coach is the person who sits beside you on the rug. Not to build it for you. Not to tell you how to build it. To help you figure out what will help you move forward, and keep building.
Coaching works in conjunction with other forms of ADHD support such as medication, therapy, or productivity systems. It does not compete with them, it does not replace them.

adhdContent.text: ↔️ ADHD coaching closes the gap between knowing what to do and doing it — the defining frustration of ADHD.
🪡 It is a custom-tailored partnership because each ADHD brain actually works differently and presents different challenges.
🔍 Coaching isn't telling you what to do or how to do it. It's helping you discover what will enable you do the work.

subsections.note: May wish to add a tooltip for reflective-inquiry
subsections.title: A Different Kind of Support

defaultContent.text: It helps to know how coaching differs from the other ADHD support modalities.

Medication addresses the neurobiology directly. It clears the floor so you can begin to build. It does not teach you how to proceed next. Therapy works on emotional wounds and challenges that often travel alongside ADHD (such as anxiety or depression). Productivity systems or life hacks often invite you to adapt your brain to someone else's methods. They tell you how to build, the way they did.

Traditional life coaching comes close in similarities of reflective-inquiry. However, ADHD coaching is rooted in the neurobiology of the ADHD brain. It acknowledges the ADHD brain is fundamentally different in its operation of regions that affect motivation, focus, and self-regulation.

adhdContent.text: ◾Medication addresses neurobiology
◾Therapy heals emotional wounds
◾Productivity tools invite your brain to use particular techniques
◾ADHD Coaching is grounded in ADHD brain science and focuses on execution.

subsections.note: Standard subsection — bullets in ADHD mode.
subsections.title: How Coaching Works

defaultContent.text:
If you've never been in a coaching relationship, it can sound vague.
"We talk about your goals." Sure. But that describes approximately everything from a job interview to a fortune cookie.😊

Coaching is a conversation. A curious one, with lots of questions. Much of the session lives in inquiry, not instruction. This is the part that surprises people. A coach doesn't hand you an action plan or efficiency hacks. The questions are the work.

At the start, there's a fair bit of excavation: mapping patterns, understanding your defaults, discovering strengths you draw on naturally. Over time, the sessions shift. As awareness and understanding deepen, you start to notice and strategize by yourself.
That shift — from coach-assisted to increasingly self-directed — is the whole point. The coaching relationship is designed to make itself less necessary over time.

adhdContent.text: The work happens through questions, not instructions
The coaching process surfaces your own insight; the coach doesn't had you a plan
Over time, coaching builds self-directed awareness — you start catching patterns earlier, needing less external scaffolding.

subsections.note: ADHD content is an image pair (TheirBuildOrder + MyBuildOrder2) — hardcoded in HTML. adhdContent here is ignored by the renderer. To edit the images or labels, update the HTML directly.
subsections.title: Adapt to the Way Your Brain Works

defaultContent.text: "Just be more organized" is like being told to build a castle without any baseplates. Technically possible. Deeply miserable. Not the point.

We don't force your brain through a linear 1-to-100 manual if you work better building the dragon first. We honor how your brain actually moves — hyperfocus, creative leaps, nonlinear everything — and design your Build Station around that. The pieces you need most? Always within reach.

subsections.title: Why Self-Awareness + Self-Compassion Come First

defaultContent.text: You step on a stray brick in the dark. You curse the brick. You turn on the light and see the mess. You curse yourself. You meant to put them away. You didn't.

Here's the thing — everyone leaves bricks out. Everyone. But for us, it never feels like a minor oversight or an understandable remnant after an exhausted day. It feels like proof. Proof that we are the mess, not the floor.
That's the part worth examining — not the brick, not even how the mess came to be — but the story we've been telling ourselves about what that means.

adhdContent.text: "Getting stuck isn't a character flaw."

subsections.note: Standard subsection — emoji bullets in ADHD mode (emoji act as the visual marker, CSS hides the default LEGO stud).
subsections.title: What Coaching Can and Can't Do

defaultContent.text: A coach is a precision tool — like a brick separator. It makes the hard parts easier. It doesn't do the snapping for you.

Coaching can help you find a better way to organize the pieces. It won't replace therapy, and it won't replace medication. We can't change the pieces you were given — it's a non-standard set — but we can change the way you arrange them.
What coaching will do is help you figure out an arrangement that actually works — and help you stop waiting for the "right" moment to start.

adhdContent.text: ✅ Helps you find a better way to organize the pieces
✅ Helps you stop waiting for the "right" moment to start
✅ Changes the way you arrange what you've got
❌ Won't replace therapy
❌ Won't replace medication
❌ Can't change the pieces you were given
❌ Can't help you win the lottery

subsections.note: Standard subsection — emoji bullets in ADHD mode (emoji act as the visual marker, CSS hides the default LEGO stud).
subsections.title: When Coaching Makes Sense

defaultContent.text: You're ready when the to-build pile is touching the ceiling and you're tired of just looking at the boxes. What used to bring joy now just brings dread.
You don't need more sets. You need a system to open the ones you already have.
Coaching is for the builder who's done waiting for the "perfect" moment — and is ready to see something on the shelf.

adhdContent.text: 🧱 The to-build pile is touching the ceiling
📦 You're tired of just looking at the boxes
😶 What used to bring joy now just brings dread
🔧 You're not sure you're ready — but you're tired of waiting to feel ready
